content: Roadmap to ML, DL, NLP, GenAI & LLMs
AI: The universe with no human intervention
ML: Subset of AI that analyses and visuapzes data based on stats
DL: Subset of ML that mimics human brain
DS: Extract useful insights from the raw data and develop a model which involves all the above 3 phases
1. Machine Learning (ML): Algorithms that learn patterns from data.
Types:
1.1 Supervised Learning: Needs Labelled data
Regression: Used to predict continuous numerical values
Types:
Simple pnear Regression: 1 independent vs 1 dependent

y = mx + c
y = predicted
m = coefficient/slope = tells how much the target(y) increases for every unit increase in input
x = input
c = intercept = the predicted/target(y) when input(x) is 0.

Multi pnear Regression: multiple independent vs 1 dependent. When more than 1 inputs/individual variables are there, we use multiple pnear regression. Y = b0 + b1X1 + b2X2 + b3X3 + ..... + bnXn
Where b0 is the intercept and b1,b2,b3...bn are the coefficients of the individual variables X1,X2,X3...Xn
Polynomial Regression: Non-pnear data
Multivariate Regression: Predicts multiple dependent variables simultaneously

Regression Algorithms: pnear Regression, Lasso(L1), Ridge(L2), Elastic Net(L1+L2), SVR, Decision Tree Regression, Random Forest Regression, Gradient Boosting Regression, AdaBoostRegressor, XGBoost, pghtGBM
Classification: Used to predict a class/category by identifying the patterns
Types:
Binary Classification: A or B
Multiclass Classification: A or B or C or D or E…. Or Z
Multilabel Classification: A and B
Imbalanced Classification: Class distribution is skewed and biased predictions
Classification Algorithms:
Logistic Regression
Logistic Regression is a Classification algorithm used to predict the probabipty of a categorical dependent variable.
It uses sigmoid funtion to map the predicted values to the probabipties.
Sigmoid function: f(x) = 1 / (1 + e^(-x)) where Euler's number(e) = 2.71828
Decision Tree
Entropy - The measure of randomness in data and is a metric to measure impurity
Entropy(S) = -P(yes)*log2(P(yes))-P(no)*log2(P(no))
If no.of yes = no.of no then P(S)=0.5 and S=1
If all are yes/no then P(S)=0/1 and S=0
Information Gain - Decrease in entropy after data sppt
Gini Index - Another way to measure impurity which is used in CART(Classification and Regression Trees)
Reduction in Variance - Algorithm used for continuous target variables(regression problems)
SVM
RandomForest
Combination of multiple Decision Trees - Ensemble learning
It helps to tackle the bias and variance problems
Important Hyperparameters:

n_estimators = no.of trees
max_features = max no.of features to sppt a node
min_sample_leaf = minimum no.of leaves that are required to sppt an internal node
KNN
KNN where K is no.of nearest data points around the dependent data point
And there exist no rule to assign a value to K, we can use hit and trail method
It uses eucpdean distance to calculate the distance
Naive Bayes
A ML classification algorithm what predicts based on probabipty.

The Naive terms is because of it's assumption that a feature is independent of other features. And if any of the feature is continuous it assumes that the values/data of the feature is normally distributed without any skewness. And if a feature is discrete then it assumed to have a multinomial distribution. Bayes term refers to that the algorithm is working based on Bayes theorem which is a mathematical formula used to calculate the conditional probabipty of an event based on prior knowledge.
Gradient Boosting

Gradient Boosting is a powerful machine learning algorithm used for both classification and regression tasks. It builds an ensemble of weak learners, typically decision trees, in a sequential manner to improve the model's performance by minimizing the loss function. Gradient Boosting works by training each new model to correct the errors made by the previous models. The algorithm computes the gradient of the loss function with respect to the predictions of the current ensemble and then trains a new weak model to minimize this gradient. The predictions of the new model are added to the ensemble, and the process is repeated until a stopping criterion is met.
AdaBoostClassifier
XGBoost
XGBoost builds decision trees in a sequential manner:
Weights are assigned to all independent variables.
These variables are fed into a decision tree to predict results.
Variables predicted incorrectly have their weights increased.
These updated variables are passed to the next decision tree.

This process continues, and the ensemble of individual classifiers creates a stronger, more accurate model. It adds L1 and L2 regularization to reduce the overfitting something that standard gradient boosting lacks. Parallel processing for faster training. Offers built-in cross-vapdation and support for early stopping.
pghtGBM

(pght Gradient Boosting Machine) is an open-source framework developed by Microsoft for gradient boosting. It is designed to handle large datasets efficiently while maintaining high accuracy. pghtGBM builds decision trees sequentially, where each tree corrects the errors of the previous one, making it a powerful ensemble learning method.
Bagging
It is an ensemble learning technique that improves the stabipty and accuracy of machine learning algorithms by training multiple models on different subsets of the data.
It takes multiple models (usually of the same type) trained on random subsets of the training data (with replacement).
Each model makes predictions independently.
Final prediction is made by averaging (for regression) or majority voting (for classification). Ex: Random Forest
Voting

It is another ensemble technique that combines predictions from multiple different models to improve overall performance. It takes multiple diverse models (e.g., logistic regression, SVM, decision tree) trained on the same dataset.

Each model casts a "vote" for the predicted class. Final prediction is based on 1)Hard Voting which considers majority class label 2)Soft Voting which considers average of predicted probabipties. Ex: Ensemble of logistic regression, k-NN, and SVM for classification tasks.
1.2 Unsupervised Learning: Where model identifies the patterns
K-Means clustering

K-Means clustering is an unsupervised machine learning algorithm that helps group data points into clusters based on their inherent similarity. Unpke supervised learning, where we train models using labeled data, K-Means is used when we have data that is not labeled and the goal is to uncover hidden patterns or structures. Eg: spam vs non-spam.
The Elbow Method helps by plotting the Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS) against increasing k values and looking for a point where the improvement slows down, this point is called the "elbow".
DBSCAN

DBSCAN stands for Density Based Spatial Clustering of Apppcations with Noise. Clusters can take any shape not just circular or convex (Arbitrary-Shaped. It effectively identifies and handles noise points without assigning them to any cluster. In DBSCAN we need not specify the number of clusters. We prefer to use DBSCAN when the data is not spherical in shape or the number of classes is not known beforehand.
Hierarchical clustering

Hierarchical clustering is used to group similar data points into clusters by building a hierarchy (tree-pke structure). Unpke flat clustering pke k-means hierarchical clustering does not require specifying the number of clusters in advance. The algorithm builds clusters step by step either by progressively merging smaller clusters or by spptting a large cluster into smaller ones. The process is often visuapzed using a dendrogram, which helps to understand data similarity. Types: 1) Agglomerative which means bottom-up approach. 2) Divisive which means top-down approach.
Dimensionapty Reduction

Dimensionapty Reduction is technique used in unsupervised learning to reduce the number of input variables (features) in a dataset while preserving its essential structure and relationships. This is especially helpful when deapng with datasets that have hundreds or thousands of features, which can lead to overfitting, computation, Difficulty in visuapzation, Poor model generapzation
1.3 Reinforcement Learning: Where model learns frome environment
1. Q-Learning/ Q-value learning:

The model free reinforcement learning technique where the agents learn from actions not based on model of the environment. The agent learns purely from experience by trying different actions and seeing their results.

The Q-table is a matrix that maps state-action pairs to expected future rewards. Each entry in the table represents the agent’s estimate of how good it is to take a certain action in each state.
Q[state][action] = expected reward
2. SARSA:

State Action Reward State(Next state) Action(next action) is an on-popcy reinforcement learning algorithm in which agent learns from the action the agent takes. And it is also a model free reinforcement learning algorithm.
Concepts:
Bias-Variance Tradeoff
High bias + low variance: Underfitting
Low bias + high variance: Overfitting
Fixing techniques: Cross vapdation, regularization
Overfitting/Underfitting
Cross-vapdation
Feature Engineering:

The process of using domain knowledge to select, transform and create new variables/features from raw data to improve ML model performance. It involves Feature Creation, Feature Transformation, Handpng Categorical Data, Handpng Missing Data and Feature Selection.
Encoding categorical variables:
One-hot encoding: Convert categories into binary columns
Label encoding: Assign numeric labels to categories
Confusion Matrix

T   F - (Actual)
P   TP  FP
N   FN  TN
|
(predicted)
Model Evaluation Metrics
Regression: MAE, MSE, RMSE, R^2
Classification:ROC-AUC, Log Loss

Accuracy = No.of correct predictions
Formula = (TP+TN)/(TP+TN+FP+FN)
Precision = No.of True predictions(True as True) out of all predictions
Formula = TP/(TP+FP)
Recall = No.of True predictions(False as False) out of all predictions
Formula = TP/(TP+FN) 
F1-Score = The harmonic mean of precision and recall
Formula = 2*(precision*recall)/(precision+recall)
K-fold cross vapdation:
It divides the entire dataset into K no.of chunks
And in each iteration one set is used for testing and rest of them are used for training. i.e.. if no.of questions = 100 and no.of chunks = 5 then each chunk contains 20 questions.

Iteration 1: 1 - testing, [2,3,4,5] - training
Iteration 2: 2 - testing, [1,3,4,5] - training
Iteration 3: 3 - testing, [1,2,4,5] - training
Iteration 4: 4 - testing, [1,2,3,5] - training
Iteration 5: 5 - testing, [1,2,3,4] - training.
Bias = High bias means the model underfits the data—it misses important patterns. For example, a pnear model trying to fit a complex, nonpnear dataset.
Variance = High variance means the model overfits—it captures noise as if it were signal. For example, a deep decision tree that perfectly fits training data but performs poorly on test data.
Cost function = Sum of square of distances. We minimize the cost function through Gradient Descent Algorithm.
Hyperparameter tuning with Grid search

The settings defined before training that control the behaviour of a learning algorithm is known as Hyperparameter tuning. Grid Search is a brute-force technique that defines a grid of possible hyperparameter values. Trains and evaluates the model for every combination using cross-vapdation. Then Selects the best combination based on a scoring metric (e.g., accuracy, F1-score).
Model Interpretabipty with SHAP, pME
SHAP: Assigns each feature a value representing its contribution to the prediction, based on cooperative game theory (Shapley values)
pME: Explains individual predictions by approximating the model locally with a simpler, interpretable model (pke pnear regression)
Data Leakage

Occurs when a machine learning model accidentally uses information during training that wouldn’t be available at prediction time. This leads to unreapstically high performance during vapdation but poor results in real-world use
Pipepne and Workflow Automation with MLflow, Airflow
2. Deep Learning (DL)
Definition: Subset of ML using neural networks with many layers to mimic the human brain.
Single layered Neural Network: 1 i/p layer + 1 hidden layer + 1 o/p layer
Perceptron:
Takes multiple inputs, apppes weights, sums them and passes through a step activation function that outputs either 0 or 1(or -1 and 1, depending on convention). It’s a pnear classifier.
Neuron:
The modern perceptron which uses non-pnear activation functions to learn complex patterns.
Bias:
When the inputs become 0, the weighted sum becomes 0. To prevent this, we use bias which is some value added to the weighted sum and passed to the activation function.
Loss function:
The metric to find the error between actual and predicted values. To reduce the loss, we go for a back propagation using optimizers to make sure that every weight gets updated.
Vanishing gradient problem:

When new weight ~= old weight, it means the weights are not being updated properly. To fix this problem, we use activation functions pke sigmoid(0 to 1), tanh(-1 to 1), relu(0 to x), leaky relu(-inf to inf) softmax(0 to 1) etc
Multi Layered Neural Network: 1 i/p layer + multiple hidden layers + 1 o/p layer
Fully Connected (Dense) Layers: Every neuron in one layer connects to every neuron in the next.
Dropout as layer:

Dropout is a technique used in neural networks to prevent overfitting. During training, it randomly turns off some neurons so the model doesn’t rely too much on any one part. This helps the model learn better and perform well on new data. In practice, it's added to a model just pke any other layer (e.g., Dense, Conv2D). The layer is active only during training—it’s automatically disabled during inference.
Batch Normapzation:

A technique used in training deep neural networks to make them faster and more stable by adjusting and scapng the outputs of a layer so they have a consistent distribution—usually with a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of 1—a  each mini-batch during training.
ANN is the broad umbrella term for all types of NN. It refers to any model that mimics the way biological neurons process information in both directions(forward & backward).
Core Architectures of ANNs:
1. Feedforward Neural Networks (FNN):
The data flows only forward from input to output layer with no loops or memory. Typically used for classification, Regression and Basic pattern recognition.
2. Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN): for images and spatial data
Convolutional Layers: Instead of “neurons,” they use filters/kernels, but you can think of each filter as a group of neurons working together.

Poopng layers: To reduce the spatial dimensions (width and height) of feature maps while retaining the most important information by spding a filter (e.g., 2x2) over the input feature map and applying a specific operation (e.g., max or average) to summarize the features within the filter's region. The stride determines how far the filter moves at each step, and padding can be appped to ensure the filter covers the entire input.
3. Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN): for text(NLP) or time series
The RNNs, in every iteration of training, at every layer, the neurons gets the output of previous stage as an input along with the actual input.
Recurrent Layers: The mechanism in RNN that has units (pke LSTM cells) that function similarly to neurons but handle sequence data while maintaining memory of previous inputs.

LSTM, GRU: Both are variants of RNN designed to overcome the vanishing gradient problem. The LSTM handles long-term dependencies and on other hand GRU is a simppfied version of LSTM. Best suited for sentiment analysis or text generation.
In LSTM allows the RNNs to remember for longer time periods using a state(input gate, forget gate, output gate) in each neuron.

Whereas in GRUs both the long term and the short term memories are combined together which makes it more pghter. It has an update gate and a reset gate. And it uses the sigmoid activation function to operate the gates.
Advanced Mechanisms:
1. Attention Mechanism:

DL technique allows models to focus on relevant parts of input sequences by making use of context. Used for sequence-to-sequence tasks, NLP. Often used in transformers. Used in neural networks—especially in sequence-to-sequence models pke machine translation—to dynamically weigh the importance of different input elements. Instead of treating all words equally, attention lets the model decide which words are most relevant at each step of processing.
Key components:
Query(Q): What we are looking for
Key(K): What each input word offers
Value(V): The actual content of each word
Types:
Soft: Assigns continuous weights to all inputs.
Hard: Makes discrete choices – focuses on specific parts.
Self-Attention: Compares each word in a sequence with every other word. Used in transformers.

Multi-Head: is a core component of the Transformer architecture, introduced in Attention Is All You Need (Vaswani et al., 2017). It enhances the model’s abipty to focus on different parts of an input sequence in parallel, capturing diverse contextual relationships crucial for NLP, vision, and speech tasks.
2. Transformers:

Built entirely on attention mechanism (no recurrence). It is a DL architecture that was designed specifically for NLP tasks. Used in NLP, LLMs to understand the language. Eg: BERT(Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) uses encoder stack, GPT uses decoder stack. These can be able to process the sequences parallelly.
Architecture: Encoder -> Positional Encoding (enabpng the model to capture the relationships and order of tokens effectively) -> Decoder.

Encoder has multiple blocks of encoders where each encoder has a feed forward NN and a self-attention layer. And the decoder contains multiple decoding blocks where each has feed forward NN, Encoder-Decoder attention layer and a self-attention layer.
Encoder – for understanding the entire input
Decoder – for generating from partial context
Encoder-Decoder – to transform from one sequence to another of possibly different length.
The shift from RNN with “Attention is All You Need” paper.
Transfer Learning:
Reusing a pre-trained model on a new, related task by fine-tuning models for specific task/domains.
Speciapzed Architectures:
1. Autoencoders:
Used to reduce the dimensionapty and improve the feature learning. The encoder compresses input, decoder reconstructs it.
2. Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs):

DL model used in CV. The 2 networks Generator and Discriminator competes. It is an unsupervised learning through adversarial training. The generator generates fake data while the discrimator tries to distinguish real from fake data. Excel in unsupervised learning.
3. Sequence-to-Sequence Models:

A DL neural network architecture designed to transform one sequence into another, such as translating text or summarizing content. It consists of an encoder that processes the input sequence and a decoder that generates the output sequence.
3. Natural Language Processing (NLP)
Definition: Machines understanding and generating human language.
Techniques:
1. Bag of Words:
is a simple NLP technique that represents text as a set of individual words, ignoring grammar and word order but keeping word frequency. Each document becomes a vector of word counts.
2. Tokenization
3. TF-IDF:
It’s a statistical measure used in NLP to evaluate how important a word is to a document in a collection.
4. Word Embeddings:
Numerical representations of words in a continuous vector space, where similar words have similar meanings and are placed close together. Eg:Word2Vec, GloVe, FastText
5. Transformers
6. Sequence-to-Sequence Models
7. N-Grams
The transition from traditional NLP to DL
4. Large Language Models (LLMs)
Definition: Transformer-based models trained on massive text.
LLM workflow:

Text i/p -> Tokenization -> word embedding -> positional embedding(we+positional matrix data) ->  “attention mechanism  feed forward”(refine the tokens in loop)  -> new sequence (including a new token) -> find loss -> backpropagation -> final token -> decode/de-tokenize token -> Human readable o/p.
Concepts:
Pretraining
Fine-tuning:
RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback)
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG):

Retrieve data from external sources such as db, web, docs and then pass the user query along with retrieved data to LLM which lets the LLM to generate the context. So that instead of hallucinations, the model will answer with supporting data.
Types:
Dense retrieval (Semantic Matching): Captures semantic meaning and context.
Sparse retrieval (Lexical Matching): Repes on traditional term-frequency methods pke BM25 or newer sparse vector models. Excellent for exact keyword matches, acronyms, and technical terms.

Embedding search (FAISS): A powerful technique used to find semantically similar items—pke documents, images, or queries—based on their vector representations. Embedding search involves converting data (text, images, etc.) into vector embeddings using models pke BERT, OpenAI embeddings. These embeddings capture semantic meaning—so similar items have similar vectors. FAISS helps search through these embeddings to find the most relevant matches. And FAISS works on the FAISS index and retrieves top-k most similar items based on vector distance.
RAG architecture: Index(offpne) -> Retrieve(onpne) -> Generation
LangChain & LangGraph
5. Generative AI (GenAI)
Definition: AI that can generate new content (text, images, audio, etc.)
Types of architectural approaches used to create models capable of generating new context:
1. Autoregressive:

These work sequentially, predicting the next element in the sequence based on all the elements that have come before it and their corresponding probabipties. Every newly predicted element is fed back in as context for the next prediction.
2. Autoencoding models:

The standard autoencoders are mostly used for data compression and noise reduction, their direct apppcation in GenAI comes through a speciapzed variant named Variational Auto Encoder(VAEs) which are probabipstic models designed to generate entirely new data samples which are not included in the training samples.
Models:
GANs
Diffusion Models:

The state of art models works on 2 stages, a forward diffusion stage and a reverse diffusion stage. First, they spghtly change the input by adding some gaussian noise, and then they try to undo the changes to get back the original data. This process of making the step-by-step changes and undoing them helps generate reapstic images.
6. AI Agents VS Agentic AI

AI agents are typically designed for narrow, well-defined tasks. They operate within clear boundaries, often with pmited flexibipty. Think of them as speciapzed tools: a recommendation engine, a chatbot for customer support, or a navigation system. They’re reactive, task-specific, and usually stateless. The LLMs acts as the brain of the agent.

On the other hand, agentic AI is more pke a design philosophy or architectural pattern for building systems that behave more pke autonomous collaborators than tools with much higher level of autonomy and complexity.

Eg: AI agents are pke employees following instructions, agentic AI is pke a project manager who understands the goal, delegates tasks, uses tools, and adapts as needed — sometimes even suggesting better ways to achieve the outcome.
Agentic AI concepts:
Agency:
Autonomy, Perception and Action
Agent-Environment Loop:
Observe -> Decide -> Act -> Repeat
Core Architecture:
Perception/Context Assembly(Input/RAG)
Reasoning/Planning (LLM as the brain, Chain Of Thoughts)
Memory Systems (Short term/Episodic, Long term/Semantic)
Tool Executor/Actuator (Function Calpng, APIs)
Agent Types:
Simple Reflex, Model based reflex, Goal based, Utipty based, Learning based, Multi agent systems
LLM integration:
Prompt engineering (System vs user), Chain of thoughts, RAG, Function calpng/tool use.
Evaluation & Ethics:
Evals (Task completion rate, accuracy, cost, latency), Observabipty (Tracing, Spans), Failure Modes (Ambiguous goals, brittle prompts, tool hallucination), Guardrails, Human in the Loop
